          Photographs and Clip Art
          Somc:1imc11 )'OUhnvc to represen1something visunlly using im.ngcs of mn1crfol in Lhc Y..'Orld
          (hurn.uns, animals, machines. cLc,). and phocogrnphs and clip an c,in help you achieve Lhill
          purpose. As you work with photogr.ipki: and clip art. you will encounter two different lite
          ronnals: vL-ctor and r1t.Stcr (also known tis bitmap). Voctor uuagL-s arc: made up ol' bask

          fommla dicln1C$ lhc shape. size. und color of the oh1cc1, Vector images cnnno1 d1spl11y the
          detailed nniurnl qu;1li1ics of photogrnphs,
              Rll,;;tcr images m-0 com1m11cd ofrowi1 nnd columni1 of1iny pixels (n word comcd from

           Chapter Summary

          When communicating with fiQcial medin1 you hnve nlmost endless oppommilie-. to stny in

                     One Writes All                     Each Writes Part

                        All Write All
                                                               ~[i)~
                       tftti f0 0t      Team Writing Projects
                    One Write, All. One person writes the entire document. Team
                    gives feedback. Consistent style but unfair workload. Works
                    well for short documents.
                    All Writ• All. TM entire team composes togtther. Consistent
                    style, but can be tedious, frustrating, and Inefficient. May
                    work reasonably well for very short documents.
                    Each Writes Part. Team member1 divide and delegate the
                    writing and share in giving feedback. Time efficient and equi•
                    table workloads, but inconsistent writing style. Works well for
                    long documenb. Delays can <Xcur when deadlines aren 1t met.

           Chapter Summary

          Rcpons arc ,mponnnt for nll profcssionnl orga1uzauons. Reports differ from ord1n.u·y
          intCl'J)ersonul messages In M!Vcml ways. Ordinnty businc..s mess.ages arc ~horl, informal,
          tr.tn.<;nctional documents wntten uboul a wlde vuncty ot' day•to•dny ncllv1t1cs. Business
          reports arc often the result of unportanl inveshgalions. They provide ,11tal lnfornu1lion for
          manngc~ and arc longer and more formnl 1tu111 rmuine mcsrw1gcs.
             Businc,is rcpon,i can be \vril1cn m one of1hr« wuy,i. F'iri11. one person can do all the

          ~quires spec bl nw.nasemcnt to make sure the team works well logcther ond to ensure the
          quality ofl hc fina lrcpor1.

          complete a MCS plun clarify the purpose. nnalyrs:. 1hc nudicncc. unalyze 1hc contex1. 11nd

               Most l'l.'l)Ol1s can be c:luss,liL'tl into one of four L~1tegoricll. ~Int progl'css rep011s updn1c

         Designing Visual Aids

           Balance. All the text nnd visuals on a slule or page should be-\!isually b.1lane<:d either
          S)'mmc1ricnll)' or osymmctricnlly. Symmctricnl bnluncc 1uoduccs n slide 1hn1 looks cvcnl)'
          balanced, wi1h the left nnd nght sides being mirror images ofcnch other. Vli1h asymmclricnl
          balance, the lcfl and right sidc11 ore nOI visually the same. Ncvc11hclcsl!. in some Cll~Cll there
          may std I be balance, such as IV.-'O srnnll items on lhc 1ighl lo oountcr-balnnec one large: hem
          on the: lefi. •ro the human eye, symmctricnl layout looks more fonnnl. but less mtercslins;
          asymmetric.al lil)'OUI looks less fonnal. hut more intcrc:stmg (sec Figure 11.13).

            Videos

           Sometimes n video is ll good wuy lO convey 1hc informmion you wun110 1ncsen1. A video

          Plan with PACS
          Before you begin crcatmg II prescn101ion, remember 10 complete the l lur imponont PACS
          plannmg steps.

          Purpose. First. dnrify your purpose. Know why you arc presenting and what you want tu

               Al er clarifying the purpose. crcntc a good working 1illc. 011cn one of1hc .SW21I words
          wo1'kll well os the opening wonl, such all "llow to Implement the New Procesll orlalnndling
          Customer Complahu:s" or "Why We Should Develop a Working Rclotlonsh1p w11h J-\UC
          Company." Luter you might develop .i more catchy title. such as "WinnIns Back Our ClL"·
          lomcrs- How to Handle Customer Complaints." Notice that the subtitle uses the specific
          SW2H worcl:i, while the main 1i1le is more crcntive. One wny 10 create a title i$ 10 use the
          topic for1hc m11111 litlc imd the purpose for 1hc sublitle, Keep the purpose o f the prcsc1m11ion
          upJ)crmost m your mind throughout the cnurc pkmmng phosc ond throughout your achwl
          prc:-scntouon. Moke ?i.Urc: each dcmcnl of your p1tscnl11tion ?i.UPJ>Ol1S the ecnu"JI purpo.1:e,
          .ind develop your prcsenlaticm so it Dnswers oll what. so-what, and now-what questions.


          Audience. Next, annlyzc the 11udicnoe. mclucling their demographi~ nnd psychog.raph•
          ics. Find oul who will he aucnding (1hcirorgnniw1ionnl 11fliliation,knowledgc of the topic.

          Sentence Parts
          1'hc mnin ~•rts of scmcnc:cs c:an bo bmkcn i1110 lwo c1ucgorics: {I) 1iubjc:c1s, verbs. nnd
          complemcnl!i nnd (2) phr1L.;cs 1Lnd clauses. mi upluinod in 1hc f()llowing sections.


          Subjects, Verbs, and Complements. A sentence is a group of words expressing an
          idea. Most ideas h..ive three mnin demcnls:


          •   Suhj1.'tl: the thing 1hn1i~doing ~mclhmg or btins somc1hing
          ■   ~: wh:u the subject is doing or being.
          ■   Ct,mphwem: lhc oompld ion of the idea ~lal1cd by the subjl'Ct oml vc,rb

              Consider the fo llowing scntcrn:.c-: Ro:,tcr dan:c '" St. Ln11is.


          • Suhjt•tt: Roger {the thing 1ha1 did 50mcthing)
          ■ ~ : drove (the 11ction)
          • Cm11ple11wm: to St Louis {lhc rcsc or the ideu ~t.nrtcd b)' the subject ond \'crb)

             When you have- more than one- subJccLor vc1b. 11 is c.illc:d a compound subJcct or

          Agreement and Reference
          Agrccmcml rdcrs lo L'Otll!IS!cnc:-y among the variOlL'- pa1t.11 of o sentence. Sentence clements
          must agree m three mujor ways: ( I} number- singular or plural, (2} person- Orsi, second.
          or third. and (3) g_cnder- mascullnc-, feminine, or neute.r(neither). In each of >•our messag-
          es, be sure to m.ain1ain a consistent point of view within each of these three writing a:;p,eclr..

          I. Numbrr
             - Singulnr: one person or thing: e.g., "Ke11 iic my brother."
             - J•turul: more th.un one person or thing; e.g., " lfl' arc oompctin~ on Saturday."
          2. l'crsun
                  1-i rst: the wriler or spL-.1ker; e.g.• "I am the speaker."
                  S(ccmd; the pcoon lo whom the message 1s being wnnen nr spokcnj e,g,. ...You arc
                going to liko lhis movie,"
                Third: someone or something cl11c: e.g .. "Slie is going to call me back,"
          ). Ct11d<'r
                   Masculine: male (e.g.... "lie cannot koep hl\· appointment ..)
                   feminine: female (e.g.• "She hun lwr ankle.'')
                   Neuter: neither/either male or fematc (e.g... "'The tlnl/1,r found ifs way b.1d: into
                   mywallcc.")

                   sign~• memo, you muy put your initials t,,c-,;iclc your nnmc in the heading lines. or you may
                   sign 111 1hc bo11om ltl1 after the last line of 1c:<t.

                   1ion, such as fim1ncialdat11. If only one 11p1lendix iii included, it i11 nol gi\'Cll 11n idcnlilicat1on
                   letter. If more dtnn one appendix: is indudcd. the appcndi.:tc§ arc designated Il; Appendix: A,
                   Appendix U, and so forth. 'J'hc genera.I formal of uppcmlixcs 1s the same us the mam report
                   or proposal, Including typography. sp.1cing, visual design. and pagination.
